examples.explanation: Graph has 3 nodes: 0→1, 1→2, 2→0. Start with node 0: DFS from 0 visits 0 (start), then 1, then 2, then back to 0 (found cycle). Mark 0 as in cycle. Node 1: DFS from 1 visits 1 (start), then 2, then 0, then back to 1 (found cycle). Mark 1. Node 2: DFS from 2 visits 2 (start), then 0, then 1, then back to 2 (found cycle). Mark 2. All nodes are in cycles.
Graph: 0→1, 1→2, 2→none. Node 0: DFS from 0 visits 0, 1, 2. No return to 0. Node 1: DFS from 1 visits 1, 2. No return to 1. Node 2: DFS from 2 visits 2 only. No return to 2. No cycles found.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^2)
approaches.summary: For each node, perform DFS to detect if it can reach itself via outgoing edges, marking nodes in cycles. This is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Cycle Detection in Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force DFS to find all nodes that are part of cycles in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach to find all nodes that participate in cycles in a directed graph. The intuition is straightforward: for each node in the graph, we perform a DFS starting from that node to see if we can return to it through outgoing edges. If we can reach the starting node again during the DFS, then that node is part of a cycle. We repeat this process for every node in the graph. The algorithm works by maintaining a visited set for each DFS to track nodes we've encountered in the current traversal. For each node u, we start DFS from u, exploring all outgoing edges. If at any point we encounter u again (other than as the starting node), we mark u as being in a cycle. The time complexity is O(n²) because we perform DFS from each of n nodes, and each DFS could potentially visit all n nodes in the worst case (complete graph). Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and visited set. This approach connects to the key subroutine (L1) by using the DFS traversal as the core mechanism for cycle detection. Common mistakes include not resetting the visited set between different starting nodes (which would incorrectly mark nodes), forgetting to handle self-loops properly, and not considering that a node might be reachable from itself through multiple paths. The real code pattern involves nested loops: outer loop iterates through all nodes, inner DFS explores from each node.

examples.trace: Graph has 3 nodes: 0→1, 1→2, 2→0. Start with node 0: DFS from 0 visits 0 (start), then 1, then 2, then back to 0 (found cycle). Mark 0 as in cycle. Node 1: DFS from 1 visits 1 (start), then 2, then 0, then back to 1 (found cycle). Mark 1. Node 2: DFS from 2 visits 2 (start), then 0, then 1, then back to 2 (found cycle). Mark 2. All nodes are in cycles.
Graph: 0→1, 1→2, 2→none. Node 0: DFS from 0 visits 0, 1, 2. No return to 0. Node 1: DFS from 1 visits 1, 2. No return to 1. Node 2: DFS from 2 visits 2 only. No return to 2. No cycles found.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS from Single Source to Detect Self-Reachability
ladders.desc: DFS traversal from a given node to determine if it can reach itself through outgoing edges.
ladders.explanation: This is the core helper function that performs DFS from a specific starting node to check if that node can be reached again during the traversal. The intuition is that if we start at node u and during DFS we encounter u again (not counting the initial visit), then u is part of a cycle. The algorithm takes the graph and starting node u as input. We maintain a visited set to track nodes we've seen in the current DFS to avoid infinite loops. We perform DFS recursively: for the current node v, if v equals u and we're not at the initial call (we've moved through at least one edge), return True (found cycle). Otherwise, mark v as visited and recursively explore all neighbors. If any recursive call returns True, propagate True back up. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case (visiting all nodes). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. This connects to the full problem (L0) as the inner function called for each node. Common mistakes include not distinguishing between the initial visit and subsequent visits to u, not using a visited set (causing infinite recursion), and not properly backtracking visited nodes. Real code pattern is a recursive DFS with early termination on cycle detection.

examples.trace: DFS from node 0: Start at 0 (initial, don't count). Visit neighbor 1: at node 1, not equal to 0. Visit neighbor 2: at node 2, not equal to 0. Visit neighbor 0: found node 0 again! Return True.
DFS from node 0: Start at 0 (initial). Visit neighbor 1: not 0. Visit neighbor 2: not 0. No more neighbors. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Cycles via Reachability to Starting Node
ladders.desc: The central insight that a node is in a cycle if it can reach itself through outgoing edges.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental insight that drives the brute force approach: a node u is part of a cycle in a directed graph if and only if there exists a path from u back to u through the graph's edges. This is based on the definition of a cycle in graph theory. The intuition is simple: if you start at node u and can follow edges to eventually return to u, then those nodes and edges form a cycle containing u. The algorithm concept is to test this condition for each node. We don't need to find the entire cycle—just prove existence. This connects to the sub-routine (L1) as the condition checked during DFS. Time analysis: checking one node requires exploring reachable nodes (O(n)), and we check all n nodes (O(n²)). Space: O(n) to track visited nodes. Common mistakes include confusing this with undirected graphs (where cycles are different), not considering that multiple cycles might exist, or thinking we need to find the exact cycle path. Real code pattern is the logical condition in DFS: if current_node == start_node and not initial: return True.

examples.trace: From node 0, follow edge to 1, then to 2, then back to 0. Path exists: 0→1→2→0. Therefore node 0 is in a cycle.
From node 0, follow edge to 1, then to 2. No edge from 2 back to 0. No path from 0 back to 0. Therefore node 0 is not in a cycle.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with DFS
ladders.desc: Depth-first search traversal on a directed graph to explore all reachable nodes from a starting point.
ladders.explanation: This level covers DFS traversal, which is the algorithmic technique used to explore the graph. The intuition is to systematically explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, using recursion or a stack. For a directed graph, we follow outgoing edges from each node. The algorithm: start at node u, mark it as visited, then for each neighbor v of u, if v is not visited, recursively perform DFS from v. This ensures we visit all nodes reachable from u. Time complexity is O(V+E) for a graph with V vertices and E edges. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack. This connects to core logic (L2) as the mechanism to find paths from u to other nodes. Common mistakes include not handling cycles (which cause infinite recursion without visited set), not using directed edges correctly, and stack overflow for deep graphs. Real code pattern is recursive function dfs(node): mark visited, for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Start at 0, mark visited. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Go to 1: mark visited. Neighbors of 1: 3. Go to 3: mark visited. Neighbors of 3: 4. Go to 4: mark visited. Backtrack to 3, then 1, then 0. Now go to 2 from 0: mark visited. No neighbors. Done.
Start at 0, mark visited. Neighbor: 1. Go to 1, mark visited. Neighbor: 2. Go to 2, mark visited. Neighbor: 0 (already visited, stop). Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Graph Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Accessing and processing neighbors of a node in a directed graph represented as adjacency list.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of working with graph data structures. The intuition is that a directed graph is typically represented as an adjacency list: a list where index i contains a list of nodes that i points to. The basic operation is, given a node u, access graph[u] to get its neighbors, then iterate through them. For example, in Python: for neighbor in graph[u]: process(neighbor). This allows us to follow edges from u to other nodes. Time complexity for processing all neighbors of one node is O(out_degree(u)). Space is O(1) for the iteration. This connects to graph traversal (L3) as the way to explore from a node. Common mistakes include indexing errors (e.g., assuming graph is 1-indexed when it's 0-indexed), not handling empty neighbor lists, and modifying the list while iterating. Real code pattern is a simple for loop over the list at graph[node].

examples.trace: Access graph[0] = [1, 2]. Iterate: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2. Done.
Access graph[1] = [0, 2]. Iterate: neighbor 0, then neighbor 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Understanding how directed graphs are represented as adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges from one node to another. We represent it as an adjacency list: a list of lists where the index represents a node, and the value at that index is a list of nodes it points to. For example, graph = [[1, 2], [3], []] means node 0 points to nodes 1 and 2, node 1 points to node 3, and node 2 points to no nodes. The intuition is that this representation efficiently shows connections. Each sublist can be empty (no outgoing edges) or contain multiple nodes. This connects to basic operations (L4) as the data structure we operate on. Time to access a node's neighbors is O(1) for indexing. Space is O(V+E) to store all nodes and edges. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs (where edges are bidirectional), misinterpreting the list indices, or not understanding that order in neighbor lists doesn't matter. Real code pattern is defining graph as a list of lists in Python.

examples.trace: Index 0: [1] means node 0 points to node 1. Index 1: [2] means node 1 points to node 2. Index 2: [0] means node 2 points to node 0.
Index 0: [] means node 0 has no outgoing edges. Index 1: [0, 2] means node 1 points to nodes 0 and 2. Index 2: [1] means node 2 points to node 1.

approaches.name: DFS with Color Marking O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a three-color system (unvisited, visiting, visited) during DFS to detect cycles and track nodes in cycles in a single pass. It efficiently identifies all cycle nodes without extra preprocessing.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Nodes in Cycles in Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DFS with three-color marking to identify all nodes that are part of cycles in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all nodes that participate in cycles in a directed graph. The DFS with color marking approach uses a three-color system: WHITE (unvisited), GRAY (currently in recursion stack/visiting), and BLACK (fully processed/visited). During DFS traversal, when we encounter a GRAY node, we've found a cycle. The key insight is that all nodes in the current recursion stack (from the GRAY node back to the current node) are part of a cycle. We maintain a result set to collect these nodes. The algorithm performs a single DFS pass over all nodes, marking colors and tracking cycle nodes efficiently. Time complexity is O(V+E) where V is number of nodes and E is number of edges, which simplifies to O(n) for n nodes. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and color arrays. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the DFS traversal with cycle detection logic to solve the complete problem. Common mistakes include not properly resetting colors when backtracking, incorrectly identifying cycle boundaries, or missing nodes in complex cycle structures. Real code pattern: Initialize colors array with WHITE for all nodes. For each unvisited node, call DFS. In DFS: mark current node GRAY, recursively visit neighbors, if neighbor is GRAY add current path to result, mark current node BLACK when done.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors = [W, W, W, W, W], result = set(). Step 2: Start DFS at node 0. Mark 0 as GRAY. Visit neighbor 1. Step 3: Node 1: mark GRAY, visit neighbor 2. Step 4: Node 2: mark GRAY, visit neighbor 0 (GRAY - cycle detected!). Add nodes 0,1,2 to result. Visit neighbor 3. Step 5: Node 3: mark GRAY, visit neighbor 4. Step 6: Node 4: mark GRAY, visit neighbor 3 (GRAY - cycle detected!). Add nodes 3,4 to result. Step 7: Backtrack: mark 4 BLACK, 3 BLACK, 2 BLACK, 1 BLACK, 0 BLACK. Result contains all 5 nodes.

Step 1: colors = [W, W, W, W, W], result = set(). Step 2: DFS node 0: GRAY, visit 1. Step 3: Node 1: GRAY, visit 2. Step 4: Node 2: GRAY, no neighbors, mark BLACK. Step 5: Backtrack node 1: BLACK, node 0: BLACK. Step 6: DFS node 3: GRAY, visit 4. Step 7: Node 4: GRAY, visit 3 (GRAY - cycle!). Add 3,4 to result. Mark 4 BLACK, 3 BLACK. Result = {3,4}.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: DFS function that uses three colors to detect cycles and return nodes in the detected cycle.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core DFS function that traverses the graph while detecting cycles. It uses three colors: WHITE (0) for unvisited, GRAY (1) for currently in recursion stack, and BLACK (2) for fully processed. The function takes a node as input and recursively visits its neighbors. When it encounters a neighbor that is GRAY, it means we've found a cycle because we're revisiting a node that's still in the current recursion path. At this point, we can identify all nodes in the cycle by tracking the current path. The intuition is that a directed graph has a cycle if and only if DFS encounters a back edge to an ancestor in the recursion stack. Time complexity for this function is O(V+E) in worst case, but typically less for individual calls. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by providing the cycle detection mechanism that the full solution uses to identify cycle nodes. Common mistakes include not handling multiple cycles correctly, forgetting to mark nodes BLACK when done, or incorrectly identifying which nodes belong to the cycle. Real code pattern: function dfs(node, colors, graph, result, path): colors[node] = GRAY; path.append(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if colors[neighbor] == WHITE: dfs(neighbor); elif colors[neighbor] == GRAY: # Cycle detected - add all nodes from first occurrence to end of path to result; colors[node] = BLACK; path.pop().

examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): mark 0 GRAY, path=[0]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1: dfs(1): mark 1 GRAY, path=[0,1]. Step 3: Visit neighbor 2: dfs(2): mark 2 GRAY, path=[0,1,2]. Step 4: Visit neighbor 0: colors[0]=GRAY (cycle!). Add nodes from index of 0 in path (0) to end: [0,1,2] to result. Step 5: Backtrack: mark 2 BLACK, path=[0,1]; mark 1 BLACK, path=[0]; mark 0 BLACK, path=[].

Step 1: dfs(3): mark 3 GRAY, path=[3]. Step 2: Visit neighbor 4: dfs(4): mark 4 GRAY, path=[3,4]. Step 3: Visit neighbor 3: colors[3]=GRAY (cycle!). Add nodes from index of 3 in path (0) to end: [3,4] to result. Step 4: Backtrack: mark 4 BLACK, path=[3]; mark 3 BLACK, path=[].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-Color System for Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Using WHITE, GRAY, BLACK states to detect cycles during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: The three-color system is the central technique for cycle detection in directed graphs. WHITE represents unvisited nodes, GRAY represents nodes currently in the recursion stack (being visited), and BLACK represents fully processed nodes. The key insight is that in a directed graph, a cycle exists if and only if during DFS we encounter an edge to a GRAY node (a back edge). When we visit a node, we mark it GRAY. If we encounter a neighbor that's also GRAY, we've found a cycle because we're trying to revisit a node that's still in our current path. If the neighbor is BLACK, it's already been fully processed and is not part of the current path. This system works because it tracks the recursion stack state without explicitly maintaining a stack data structure. Time complexity for checking colors is O(1) per edge. Space complexity is O(V) for the color array. This feeds into L1 by providing the state tracking mechanism that enables the DFS function to detect cycles. Common mistakes include confusing the color meanings, not updating colors correctly during backtracking, or using the wrong color comparison. Real code pattern: colors = [0]*n # 0=WHITE, 1=GRAY, 2=BLACK; During DFS: if colors[neighbor] == 0: dfs(neighbor); elif colors[neighbor] == 1: # Cycle detected!

examples.trace: Step 1: Visit 0: color=GRAY. Step 2: Visit 1: color=GRAY. Step 3: Visit 2: color=GRAY. Step 4: From 2, try to visit 0: color[0]=GRAY → cycle detected! Step 5: Backtrack: 2→BLACK, 1→BLACK, 0→BLACK.
Step 1: Visit 0: GRAY. Step 2: Visit 1: GRAY. Step 3: Visit 2: GRAY. Step 4: 2 has no neighbors, mark BLACK. Step 5: Backtrack 1: BLACK, backtrack 0: BLACK. No GRAY neighbor encountered.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Representing directed graph as adjacency list and performing basic DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two parts: graph representation and DFS traversal. First, we represent the directed graph using an adjacency list - an array of lists where graph[i] contains all nodes that node i points to. This is efficient for DFS as we can quickly access neighbors. Second, we perform a basic Depth-First Search traversal without cycle detection. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition is to mimic recursion: visit a node, then recursively visit all its unvisited neighbors. This building block is essential because the cycle detection algorithm builds upon standard DFS traversal. Time complexity is O(V+E) as we visit each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and visited set. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework that the three-color system enhances for cycle detection. Common mistakes include incorrect graph representation (using wrong indices), infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without proper visited tracking, or stack overflow in deep graphs. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; # Add edges; visited = [False]*n; def dfs(node): visited[node]=True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited, neighbors=[1]. Step 2: dfs(1): mark visited, neighbors=[2]. Step 3: dfs(2): mark visited, neighbors=[3]. Step 4: dfs(3): mark visited, no neighbors. Step 5: Backtrack complete.

Step 1: dfs(0): visited, neighbors=[1,2]. Step 2: dfs(1): visited, neighbors=[3]. Step 3: dfs(3): visited, no neighbors. Step 4: Backtrack to 1 done, backtrack to 0, dfs(2): visited, no neighbors. Step 5: Backtrack complete.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tracking Visited Nodes in Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Maintaining a visited set/array to avoid reprocessing nodes during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves keeping track of which nodes have been visited during graph traversal to prevent infinite loops and redundant processing. In graph algorithms, we need a mechanism to remember nodes we've already processed because graphs can have cycles or multiple paths to the same node. The simplest approach is a boolean array or set where visited[i] = True if node i has been visited. Before processing a node, we check if it's already visited; if so, we skip it. This is crucial for both BFS and DFS. The intuition is that without visited tracking, we'd keep revisiting nodes in cycles indefinitely. Time complexity for checking visited status is O(1) with array indexing. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array. This feeds into L3 by providing the basic visited tracking that DFS uses, which later evolves into the three-color system. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark nodes as visited, checking visited status after recursion instead of before, or using wrong data structures that have poor lookup time. Real code pattern: visited = [False]*n; def traverse(node): if visited[node]: return; visited[node] = True; # Process node and neighbors.

examples.trace: Step 1: Visit 0: visited[0]=False, mark True, process neighbor 1. Step 2: Visit 1: visited[1]=False, mark True, process neighbor 0. Step 3: Try to visit 0: visited[0]=True, skip. Done.
Step 1: Visit 0: mark True, neighbors [1,2]. Step 2: Visit 1: mark True, neighbor [2]. Step 3: Visit 2 from 1: mark True. Step 4: Try to visit 2 from 0: already True, skip.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graph Basics and Node Relationships
ladders.desc: Understanding directed graphs, nodes, edges, and basic terminology.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: a directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows) connecting them. Each edge has a direction - from a source node to a destination node. Nodes represent entities, and edges represent relationships with direction. Key terminology: Node/Vertex: fundamental unit; Edge/Arc: directed connection; Outgoing edges: edges leaving a node; Incoming edges: edges arriving at a node; Neighbors: nodes connected by edges; Path: sequence of nodes connected by edges; Cycle: path that starts and ends at same node. The intuition is that directed graphs model asymmetric relationships (like web links, dependencies). This foundation is essential because the cycle detection problem operates on directed graphs. Time/space analysis isn't applicable here as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 by providing the graph structure that needs visited tracking. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs, misunderstanding edge direction, or incorrect node indexing. Real code pattern: Typically represented as adjacency list: graph = [[1,2], [3], []] means node 0 points to 1 and 2, node 1 points to 3, node 2 points to none.

examples.output: Understanding: Node 0 has outgoing edge to 1, node 1 has outgoing to 2, node 2 has no outgoing edges
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge 0->1: from node 0 to node 1. Step 3: Edge 1->2: from node 1 to node 2. Step 4: Node 0's neighbors: [1]. Node 1's neighbors: [2]. Node 2's neighbors: []. Step 5: Path example: 0->1->2. No cycle.

examples.output: Understanding: Mutual directed edges creating a 2-node cycle
examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: 0,1. Step 2: Edge 0->1: from 0 to 1. Step 3: Edge 1->0: from 1 to 0. Step 4: Node 0's neighbors: [1]. Node 1's neighbors: [0]. Step 5: Cycle: 0->1->0.

testCases.expected: Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: [2]; Node 2 neighbors: []
Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: [0]; Cycle exists
Node 0 neighbors: [1,2]; Node 1 neighbors: [3]; Node 2 neighbors: []; Node 3 neighbors: []
Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: [2]; Node 2 neighbors: [0]; Cycle exists
Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: [2]; Node 2 neighbors: [3]; Node 3 neighbors: [1]; Cycle exists
Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: [1]; Self-cycle at node 1
Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: []; Node 2 neighbors: [3]; Node 3 neighbors: []
Node 0 neighbors: [1,2]; Node 1 neighbors: [2]; Node 2 neighbors: []
Node 0 neighbors: [1]; Node 1 neighbors: [2]; Node 2 neighbors: [3]; Node 3 neighbors: [4]; Node 4 neighbors: [2]; Cycle exists

approaches.name: Kosaraju's SCC O(n)
approaches.summary: Find strongly connected components (SCCs) using Kosaraju's algorithm; nodes in cycles are those in SCCs with size > 1 or self-loops. This method leverages graph condensation for cycle detection.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Nodes in Cycles Using Kosaraju's SCC Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to identify all nodes that are part of cycles in a directed graph using Kosaraju's algorithm for strongly connected components.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all nodes that participate in cycles in a directed graph. A node is in a cycle if it belongs to a strongly connected component (SCC) with more than one node, or if it has a self-loop. Kosaraju's algorithm efficiently finds all SCCs in O(V+E) time using two DFS passes. The intuition is that by performing DFS on the original graph and its transpose, we can identify groups of nodes that are mutually reachable. After finding SCCs, we filter those with size > 1 (indicating cycles involving multiple nodes) or nodes with self-loops (single-node cycles). The algorithm works because the finishing order in the first DFS ensures that when we process the transpose graph in reverse order, we naturally discover SCCs. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency lists for the graph and its transpose. 2) First DFS on original graph to get finishing order. 3) Second DFS on transpose graph in reverse finishing order to mark SCCs. 4) Collect nodes from SCCs with size > 1. 5) Check for self-loops separately. Time complexity is O(V+E) for building graphs and two DFS passes. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and auxiliary arrays. Connection to L1: This level integrates the SCC finding subroutine with cycle detection logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle self-loops separately, incorrect transpose graph construction, or improper stack usage for finishing order. Real code pattern: def find_nodes_in_cycles(n, edges): graph = [[] for _ in range(n)] transpose = [[] for _ in range(n)] for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); transpose[v].append(u) visited = [False]*n; stack = [] def dfs1(u): visited[u]=True; for v in graph[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs1(v); stack.append(u) for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: dfs1(i) visited = [False]*n; scc_map = {} def dfs2(u, comp_id): visited[u]=True; scc_map[u]=comp_id; for v in transpose[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs2(v, comp_id) comp_id=0; while stack: u=stack.pop(); if not visited[u]: dfs2(u, comp_id); comp_id+=1 comp_sizes = {} for u in scc_map: c=scc_map[u]; comp_sizes[c]=comp_sizes.get(c,0)+1 result=set() for u in scc_map: if comp_sizes[scc_map[u]]>1: result.add(u) for u,v in edges: if u==v: result.add(u) return sorted(result)

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0->1, 1->2,3, 2->0, 3->4, 4->3. Transpose: 1->0, 2->1, 0->2, 3->1, 4->3. Step 2: First DFS on original: Start at 0: visit 0,1,2 (all connected), stack=[2,1,0]. Then 3: visit 3,4, stack=[2,1,0,4,3]. Step 3: Second DFS on transpose in reverse stack order: pop 3 (not visited), dfs2 marks SCC {3,4} as comp_id=0. pop 4 (visited). pop 0 (not visited), dfs2 marks SCC {0,1,2} as comp_id=1. Others visited. Step 4: SCC sizes: comp0 size=2, comp1 size=3. Step 5: Nodes in SCCs with size>1: {0,1,2,3,4}. No self-loops. Result sorted: [0,1,2,3,4].

Step 1: Build graph: 0->1,2, 1->2, 2->3, 3->0. Transpose: 1->0, 2->0,1, 3->2, 0->3. Step 2: First DFS: Start at 0: visit 0,1,2,3, stack=[3,2,1,0]. Step 3: Second DFS: pop 0 (not visited), dfs2 marks all nodes {0,1,2,3} as comp_id=0 (all mutually reachable). Step 4: SCC size=4>1. Step 5: Nodes: {0,1,2,3}. No self-loops. Result: [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Strongly Connected Components with Kosaraju's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Kosaraju's algorithm to identify all strongly connected components in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: Kosaraju's algorithm finds all strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph in O(V+E) time. An SCC is a maximal set of vertices where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex. The intuition is that by performing DFS on the original graph to get a finishing order, then DFS on the transpose graph in reverse finishing order, we naturally discover SCCs. This works because the finishing order ensures that when we process the transpose, we start from a 'sink' SCC and don't leak into others. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency lists for the graph and its transpose. 2) First DFS: Perform DFS on the original graph, pushing vertices to a stack when finished (post-order). 3) Second DFS: Process vertices in reverse order from the stack. For each unvisited vertex, perform DFS on the transpose graph to mark all vertices in the same SCC. Time complexity: O(V+E) for building graphs and two DFS passes. Space complexity: O(V+E) for adjacency lists and O(V) for visited arrays and stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the SCCs which are then analyzed for cycles. Common mistakes include incorrect transpose construction, using pre-order instead of post-order for stack, or not resetting visited array between passes. Real code pattern: def kosaraju(n, edges): graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; transpose=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); transpose[v].append(u); visited=[False]*n; stack=[]; def dfs1(u): visited[u]=True; for v in graph[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs1(v); stack.append(u); for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: dfs1(i); visited=[False]*n; scc_map={}; comp_id=0; def dfs2(u): visited[u]=True; scc_map[u]=comp_id; for v in transpose[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs2(v); while stack: u=stack.pop(); if not visited[u]: dfs2(u); comp_id+=1; return scc_map, comp_id

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0->1, 1->2,3, 2->0, 3->. Transpose: 1->0, 2->1, 0->2, 3->1. Step 2: First DFS: Start at 0: visit 0,1,2 (cycle), stack=[2,1,0]. Then 3: visit 3, stack=[2,1,0,3]. Step 3: Second DFS: pop 3, dfs2 on transpose marks {3} as comp_id=0. pop 0, dfs2 marks {0,1,2} as comp_id=1. scc_map: 0->1, 1->1, 2->1, 3->0.

Step 1: Build graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->0. Transpose: 1->0, 2->1, 0->2. Step 2: First DFS: Start at 0: visit all, stack=[2,1,0]. Step 3: Second DFS: pop 0, dfs2 marks all {0,1,2} as comp_id=0. Single SCC.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Graph Transpose and Reverse DFS Order to Find SCCs
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the core two-pass DFS mechanism with graph transpose to discover strongly connected components.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of Kosaraju's algorithm is the two-pass DFS with graph transpose. The intuition is that SCCs remain SCCs in the transpose graph (reversing all edges), but the reachability between different SCCs changes. By first doing DFS on the original graph to get a finishing order (post-order), we ensure that when we process the transpose in reverse order, we start from vertices that are in 'sink' SCCs (SCCs with no outgoing edges to other SCCs). This prevents mixing SCCs during the second DFS. Step-by-step: 1) First DFS: Traverse original graph, mark visited, and push vertices to stack when finished (after exploring all descendants). 2) Compute transpose graph by reversing all edges. 3) Second DFS: Pop vertices from stack (reverse finishing order). For each unvisited vertex, run DFS on transpose graph; all vertices reached belong to the same SCC. Time complexity: O(V+E) for two DFS passes. Space complexity: O(V+E) for adjacency lists. Connection to L1: This logic is the heart of the SCC finding subroutine. Common mistakes include pushing to stack in pre-order instead of post-order, not properly constructing transpose, or incorrect visited array management. Real code pattern: def core_scc_logic(n, edges): graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); visited=[False]*n; stack=[]; def dfs1(u): visited[u]=True; for v in graph[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs1(v); stack.append(u); for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: dfs1(i); transpose=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u in range(n): for v in graph[u]: transpose[v].append(u); visited=[False]*n; scc_ids=[-1]*n; comp_id=0; def dfs2(u): visited[u]=True; scc_ids[u]=comp_id; for v in transpose[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs2(v); while stack: u=stack.pop(); if not visited[u]: dfs2(u); comp_id+=1; return scc_ids

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->0. Step 2: First DFS: Start at 0, visit 0,1,2 (all), push post-order: after dfs1(2): stack=[2]; after dfs1(1): stack=[2,1]; after dfs1(0): stack=[2,1,0]. Step 3: Transpose: 1->0, 2->1, 0->2. Step 4: Second DFS: pop 0, dfs2 on transpose visits 0,2,1 (all), assign comp_id=0 to all. scc_ids=[0,0,0].

Step 1: Graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->1, 3->0. Step 2: First DFS: Start at 0: visit 0,1,2 (1 and 2 cycle), stack=[2,1,0]. Start at 3: visit 3, stack=[2,1,0,3]. Step 3: Transpose: 1->0,2, 2->1, 0->3, 3->. Step 4: Second DFS: pop 3, dfs2 on transpose marks {3} as comp_id=0. pop 0, dfs2 marks {0} as comp_id=1 (only reaches 0). pop 1, dfs2 marks {1,2} as comp_id=2. scc_ids=[1,2,2,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Transpose and DFS Post-order Traversal
ladders.desc: Learn to construct the transpose of a directed graph and perform DFS with post-order node recording.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two key operations: creating the transpose of a directed graph and performing DFS while recording nodes in post-order. The transpose graph is formed by reversing the direction of every edge. For example, if the original graph has an edge u->v, the transpose has v->u. This is crucial because SCCs are preserved in the transpose, but reachability between SCCs changes. DFS post-order traversal means we record a node after visiting all its descendants (i.e., when backtracking). This order helps in topological sorting and is essential for Kosaraju's algorithm to process 'sink' components first. Step-by-step: 1) Transpose construction: Iterate through all edges, and for each u->v, add v->u to the transpose adjacency list. 2) DFS post-order: Perform DFS, and after recursively visiting all neighbors, append the current node to a list or stack. Time complexity: O(V+E) for building transpose and DFS. Space complexity: O(V+E) for adjacency lists. Connection to L2: These operations are fundamental to the core two-pass logic. Common mistakes include inefficient transpose construction (e.g., O(V^2)), incorrect post-order recording (mixing with pre-order), or not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def transpose_and_postorder(n, edges): graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); transpose=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u in range(n): for v in graph[u]: transpose[v].append(u); visited=[False]*n; post_order=[]; def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for v in graph[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v); post_order.append(u); for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: dfs(i); return transpose, post_order

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0->1, 1->2. Step 2: Transpose: for edge 0->1: add 1->0; for 1->2: add 2->1. So transpose: 0:[], 1:[0], 2:[1]. Step 3: DFS post-order: Start at 0: visit 0, then neighbor 1, then neighbor 2. After dfs(2): append 2; backtrack to 1: append 1; backtrack to 0: append 0. post_order=[2,1,0].

Step 1: Graph: 0->1, 1->0, 2->3. Step 2: Transpose: 0->1 becomes 1->0; 1->0 becomes 0->1; 2->3 becomes 3->2. So transpose: 0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[], 3:[2]. Step 3: DFS post-order: Start at 0: visit 0,1 (cycle). After dfs(1): append 1; after dfs(0): append 0. Then start at 2: visit 2,3. After dfs(3): append 3; after dfs(2): append 2. post_order=[1,0,3,2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Implement DFS traversal for a directed graph using adjacency list representation.
ladders.explanation: Depth-First Search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For directed graphs, DFS helps in exploring reachability and is the basis for many advanced algorithms. The intuition is to start from a node, recursively visit all unvisited neighbors, marking nodes as visited to avoid cycles and infinite loops. Step-by-step: 1) Represent the graph as an adjacency list: for each node, store a list of its outgoing neighbors. 2) Maintain a visited array to track which nodes have been processed. 3) For each unvisited node, call a recursive DFS function that marks the node as visited and recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. Time complexity: O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity: O(V) for visited array and recursion stack (which can be O(V) in worst-case for a linear graph). Connection to L3: DFS is used for post-order traversal and graph exploration in higher levels. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (leading to infinite loops), inefficient adjacency list construction, or not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: def dfs_directed(n, edges): graph=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); visited=[False]*n; def dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for v in graph[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v); for i in range(n): if not visited[i]: dfs(i); return visited

examples.trace: Step 1: Build graph: 0->1, 1->2, 2->3. Step 2: Start DFS at node 0: mark visited[0]=True, go to neighbor 1: mark visited[1]=True, go to neighbor 2: mark visited[2]=True, go to neighbor 3: mark visited[3]=True. All nodes visited.

Step 1: Graph: 0->1, 0->2. Step 2: Start at node 0: mark visited[0]=True. Visit neighbor 1: mark visited[1]=True (no further neighbors). Backtrack to 0, visit neighbor 2: mark visited[2]=True. All visited.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Traversal
ladders.desc: Learn to represent a directed graph using adjacency lists and understand simple graph traversal concepts.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers the basics of directed graphs and how to represent them programmatically. A directed graph consists of vertices (nodes) and directed edges (arrows from one vertex to another). The most common representation for algorithmic problems is the adjacency list: for each vertex, we store a list of its outgoing neighbors. This is efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E) space). Basic traversal involves visiting vertices in some order; for example, we can iterate through all vertices and their neighbors. The intuition is that graphs model relationships, and traversal allows us to explore these relationships. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty list for each vertex (0 to n-1). 2) For each edge (u, v), append v to the list for vertex u. 3) To traverse, loop through each vertex and its neighbor list. Time complexity: O(V+E) for construction and traversal. Space complexity: O(V+E) for adjacency lists. Connection to L4: This representation is essential for implementing DFS. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in vertex indices, not handling multiple edges correctly, or using inefficient representations like adjacency matrices for large sparse graphs. Real code pattern: def graph_basics(n, edges): adj_list=[[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj_list[u].append(v); traversal=[]; for u in range(n): traversal.append((u, adj_list[u])); return adj_list, traversal

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj_list for 3 vertices: [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge 0->1: adj_list[0].append(1) -> [[1],[],[]]. Add edge 1->2: adj_list[1].append(2) -> [[1],[2],[]]. Step 3: Traverse: vertex 0 has neighbors [1]; vertex 1 has neighbors [2]; vertex 2 has neighbors [].
Step 1: Initialize: [[],[]]. Step 2: Add 0->1: [[1],[]]. Add 1->0: [[1],[0]]. Step 3: Traverse: vertex 0: [1]; vertex 1: [0].
